---
title: "About this guide"
toc: true
---

## Purpose

This guide supports annotation of images from **Planktivore**, a passive plankton imager developed at MBARI and deployed on an LRAUV. The instrument carries two optical sets, a **high-magnification** and a **low-magnification** system. Images are classified by an image classifier trained on a human-annotated set, so the quality and consistency of human labels directly determines classifier performance.

The guide has three jobs:

1. **Consistency** — give every annotator the same reference so the same organism
   gets the same label.
2. **Validation** — provide a standard to check existing labels against, as well as dispute or suggest changes.
3. **Training** — bring new annotators up to speed quickly.

## How to read a class page

Each class page has the same structure:

- **Defining characteristics** — the features that place an image in this class.
- **Distinguishing from similar classes** — the look-alikes and how to separate them. This is the single most important section for consistency.
- **Ideal images** — clean, textbook examples of what the class *should* look like.
- **Challenging images** — messy, partial, or low-contrast frames like those you will actually encounter, shown so you can recognise the class in realistic conditions.
- **References** — external keys and resources.

Click any image to open a zoomable view.

## Labelling conventions

- Label to the level the guide defines (usually **genus** for these diatoms); do not guess species unless a project-specific key is provided.
- When an image is too degraded to assign confidently, follow the project' unknown/low-quality labelling convention rather than forcing a class.
- The **high-mag** and **low-mag** optics can show the same organism very differently; each class page notes which optics it applies to.

## Reporting a problem with a label

Spotted something wrong — a specimen that looks misidentified, an image that doesn't belong in a class, an inaccurate or unclear description, or a look-alike distinction that needs sharpening? Please report it so it can be tracked and fixed.

The guide is version-controlled on GitHub, and the best way to flag a problem is to open an **issue** on the repository:

[**→ Open a new issue**](https://github.com/CeNCOOS/planktivore-labelling-guide/issues/new?template=label-problem.yml)

To help resolve it quickly, include:

- **Which class** it concerns (e.g. *Pseudo-nitzschia*), or paste the page URL.
- **Which image**, if relevant — click a thumbnail to zoom and note its filename.
- **What looks wrong**, and what it should be if you know.
- **A reference** (key, paper, or link) supporting the correction, if you have one.

Opening an issue needs a free GitHub account. If you can't create one, pass the same details to the guide maintainers and they'll file it for you.

## Contributing / updating

Content lives in `classes/<class>/meta.yml` plus `ideal/` and `challenging/` image folders. Edit the metadata or drop in images, run the build, and the HTML site, PDF, and slide deck all regenerate. See the repository `README.md` for the workflow.

Suggestion can also be made on the github repo.
